pages.raw_text:
Some diversion not on the pro-
gram was created at the open
meeting of the Central Labor
Union last night by Tom Joyce, of
the bartenders, who demanded in
formation as to whether the central
body had endorsed Mayor Daggett
for reelection. Joyce is out for Mr..
Moore and naturally the process
did not suit his idea of the general
fitness of things.


His answer was President Ma
loney's sudden adjournment Of the
meeting, whereupon Joyce proceed
ed to organize a session on the side
walk and to express himself extern
sively thereat


Strictly as a matter of fact. the
Central Labor Union did not for
mally endorse anybody. The &C
ton was taken by committees from
unions incorporated In the Central
Labor Union and the Building
Trades Alliance. Twenty-eight
unions of the 40 in town were rep
resented In the general report made
public yesterday


The Central Labor Union as a
body is not supposed to have po-
litical ideas or aspirations. 1t has
members and committees.


Last nights meeting was devoted
to addresses on labor subjects inter
spersed with music. The hall was
crowded, a number of ladies being
present. F. K. Robertson delivered
an impromptu address on the rights
Of labor; A. w. Jones spoke on the
closed shop; j. Golden on the union
and the state. A short address on
the general situation was delivered
by +. A. CoOney. and several other
speakers participated.


Contrary to expectations in the
afternoon neither Mr.. Moore nor
Mayor Daggett were present. AS
the meeting was strictly non
political both candidates were left
off the program as finally ar
